text: The surgical field reveals a distended abdomen with visible pericholecystic fluid and edema surrounding the gallbladder.

Wasn't experiencing significant abdominal pain, but I think he's just been through so much that he just has a high pain threshold. You can see him choking up in this gallbladder down by the infundibulum because there's just literally no working space in the abdomen. He has significant lymphadenopathy. He has hepatic disease, splenic disease, and he has bony mets.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) guidance to dissect the gallbladder, which is positioned relatively intrahepatic at the infundibulum. There is a visible presence of pericholecystic fluid, and the surgeon attempts to maneuver the artery out of the way due to the duct's low position.
The surgeon uses a round tip scissor to dissect the lateral portion of the gallbladder, revealing pericholecystic fluid and a completely edematous cystic plate.
The surgeon attempts to obtain the critical view of safety, revealing the back surface of the liver. Gentle cautery is applied to free the cystic plate, which is visibly edematous and vascular.
